content.text: Generics and Protocols are two advanced components of Python's type hint system that solve different but related problems in writing reusable, type-safe code.

Generics allow type definitions to be parameterized by other types. A generic function or class is a template that can work with values of many different types while still being type-checked — the type parameter is filled in concretely at each call site or instantiation. Python's built-in
are already generic types: the same
to produce type-safe collections. User-defined generics extend this pattern to custom classes and functions.

Protocols define structural interfaces — a set of methods and attributes that a type must implement to satisfy a Protocol, without requiring inheritance. This is Python's approach to duck typing with static verification. A class satisfies a Protocol if it happens to have the required methods and attributes, regardless of whether it explicitly declares that it implements the Protocol. This is sometimes called 'structural subtyping' or 'static duck typing.'

Together, Generics and Protocols form the foundation of expressive, reusable type-safe Python code. They are central to frameworks like dataclasses, Pydantic, and FastAPI, and to the type stubs provided for popular libraries.
Without generics, writing a type-safe generic function requires either duplicating code for every type or accepting

as the parameter and return type, which disables type checking. Consider a function that returns the first element of a list: if the list contains strings, the return is a string; if it contains integers, the return is an integer. Without generics, there is no way to express this relationship — the return type must be
, and callers lose type information about the return value.

Protocols address the fragility of inheritance hierarchies for defining interfaces. In traditional object-oriented programming, a function that accepts any 'drawable' object requires all drawable objects to inherit from an abstract
class. This creates tight coupling between the function and the class hierarchy. Protocols break this coupling: any class with a
Protocol, with no inheritance required. This makes Protocols ideal for typing legacy code, third-party libraries, and any situation where you cannot modify the class definitions.

In agentic AI systems, Protocols are particularly valuable for typing the pluggable components — tool handlers, memory backends, retrieval strategies — that are designed to be swapped. A Protocol defines the interface that all implementations must satisfy, and the agent framework's type annotations reference the Protocol rather than any concrete class.
A generic Stack[T] works correctly with any type T. Callers get precise type information about what they put in and take out without any code duplication.
When a generic function returns T, callers get the exact type of the value, not Any. This enables chaining of type-safe operations on the return value.
Protocols let you define interfaces without imposing inheritance. Third-party objects that happen to implement the interface automatically satisfy the Protocol.
Code that accepts a Protocol is not coupled to any concrete implementation. Swapping implementations requires no changes to the code that uses them — only to the Protocol implementations themselves.
Protocol-typed interfaces make mocking and stubbing simple. Test doubles only need to implement the Protocol's methods; they do not need to inherit from a shared base class.
checks against it at runtime, enabling dynamic dispatch based on capability.

Advanced generics with multiple TypeVars, covariance/contravariance, and bounded TypeVars can produce type signatures that are difficult to read and understand. Over-engineering the type system creates more friction than it resolves.
Complex generic hierarchies are expensive to typecheck. A codebase with many deeply nested generic types can slow mypy by an order of magnitude compared to non-generic equivalent code.
Protocol structural checking can miss intent:
Structural typing checks method signatures, not semantics. A class with a method named
that draws to a database rather than a display would satisfy a
Protocol, even though the semantics are wrong. Protocols check structure, not behavior.
Generic type parameters have variance rules that are non-obvious. A
even if Dog is a subtype of Animal, because lists are mutable. Getting variance right requires understanding type theory.
Only method presence (not signature) is checked by runtime_checkable isinstance() checks. A class that has the right method names but wrong signatures passes the runtime check but would fail mypy.

A TypeVar is a type variable — a placeholder that represents an as-yet-unknown concrete type. When mypy encounters a generic function, it infers what type the TypeVar should be bound to at each call site. If a function is declared as
tells mypy T = str, so the return type is inferred as str; calling
makes T = int, so the return type is int.
TypeVars can be bounded (constrained to a specific base class or set of types) or unconstrained (can be any type). Bounded TypeVars enable functions that use methods of the bound:
means T can be any type that implements Comparable.
A class can be made generic by inheriting from
syntax (Python 3.12). Generic classes allow the type parameter to flow from instantiation to individual method signatures, maintaining type consistency across all methods of the class.

A Protocol class defines an interface by declaring methods and attributes with their types. Any class that has those methods and attributes — matching signatures — satisfies the Protocol, regardless of whether it explicitly inherits from the Protocol class. Mypy performs structural compatibility checking at each call site.
Type parameter variance describes how a generic type relates to its parameter's subtype relationship. A type
. It is contravariant if the relationship reverses. It is invariant if neither holds.
): write-only or consumer types. A function that processes Animal can process Dog.
because you could append a Cat to it.

The diagram below illustrates how a generic class and a Protocol interact: the generic class Stack[T] maintains type safety across method calls, while the Drawable Protocol allows Circle to be used where Drawable is expected without inheritance.

attrs.alt: Generic classes with TypeVar and Protocol structural typing diagram

content.text: In the left panel, Stack[T] parameterizes all methods with the type variable T. When instantiated as Stack[int], mypy substitutes T=int throughout the class, enforcing that push only accepts int and pop only returns int. In the right panel, Circle satisfies the Drawable Protocol because it has a draw() method with a compatible signature, even though Circle does not inherit from Drawable.

Use generics when writing container, wrapper, or transformation functions that should preserve the type of their argument or contents. A Registry, Cache, or Queue class that works with any type is a natural candidate for generics.
Use TypeVar with bound when the type parameter must support specific operations. If your function calls
on the input, constrain T to

Use Protocol when defining plugin architectures, strategy patterns, or any interface that multiple independent classes should implement. The Protocol describes the capability, and any satisfying class can be used.
on Protocols when you need to check whether an object satisfies the Protocol at runtime (e.g., in a plugin loader that accepts arbitrary classes).
Avoid generics and Protocols for simple, single-type use cases. A function that only ever accepts
should be annotated as such, not as

"""Generics with TypeVar — step by step."""
from typing import TypeVar, Generic, Optional
from collections.abc import Sequence, Callable

# ─── 1. Basic TypeVar usage in functions ──────────────────────────────────────
T = TypeVar("T")

def first(items: Sequence[T]) -> Optional[T]:
    """Return first element of a sequence, or None if empty."""
    return items[0] if items else None

# mypy infers return type from the sequence element type:
x: Optional[str] = first(["a", "b", "c"])  # str | None
y: Optional[int] = first([1, 2, 3])          # int | None
z: Optional[str] = first([])                 # str | None (empty case)

# ─── 2. TypeVar with bound ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from typing import SupportsFloat

F = TypeVar("F", bound=SupportsFloat)

def to_float(value: F) -> float:
    """Convert any SupportsFloat type to float."""
    return float(value)

to_float(3)       # OK (int supports float())
to_float(3.14)    # OK (float supports float())
# to_float("3.0") # Depends: str supports float() in practice but mypy
                   # may flag it depending on stub definitions

# ─── 3. TypeVar with constraints (exactly two choices) ───────────────────────
AnyStr = TypeVar("AnyStr", str, bytes)  # can be ONLY str or bytes

def double(s: AnyStr) -> AnyStr:
    """Double a string or bytes object."""
    return s + s

result_str: str   = double("hello")  # type is str
result_bytes: bytes = double(b"hi")  # type is bytes
# double(123)  # mypy error: int doesn't match str or bytes

# ─── 4. Generic class ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class Stack(Generic[T]):
    """LIFO stack parameterized by element type."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._items: list[T] = []

    def push(self, item: T) -> None:
        """Push an item onto the stack."""
        self._items.append(item)

    def pop(self) -> T:
        """Pop and return the top item. Raises IndexError if empty."""
        if not self._items:
            raise IndexError("pop from empty stack")
        return self._items.pop()

    def peek(self) -> Optional[T]:
        """Return top item without removing it, or None if empty."""
        return self._items[-1] if self._items else None

    def is_empty(self) -> bool:
        return not self._items

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._items)

# Usage: type parameter inferred or explicit
int_stack: Stack[int] = Stack()
int_stack.push(42)
int_stack.push(100)
top: int = int_stack.pop()         # mypy knows this is int

str_stack: Stack[str] = Stack()
str_stack.push("hello")
# str_stack.push(123)  # mypy error: int is not str

# ─── 5. Generic function with multiple TypeVars ───────────────────────────────
K = TypeVar("K")
V = TypeVar("V")

def invert_dict(d: dict[K, V]) -> dict[V, K]:
    """Swap keys and values in a dictionary."""
    return {v: k for k, v in d.items()}

original: dict[str, int] = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
inverted: dict[int, str] = invert_dict(original)  # type inferred correctly

module is entirely built on Protocols (and ABCs, their predecessor). Libraries that accept 'any file-like object' or 'any mapping' use these structural types. Here is how a production data pipeline uses generics and protocols to remain flexible while being fully type-checked:
This pattern is used in production data pipelines where the source might be a database, a message queue, a CSV file, or an API response stream. The generic
Protocol allow the pipeline infrastructure to be written once and reused for any source type and record type, with full type safety throughout.
Mistake 1: Using a concrete class where Generic should be used

from typing import Any

# WRONG: accepting Any when T would be more informative
def bad_first(items: list[Any]) -> Any:
    return items[0] if items else None

# Callers lose type information:
result = bad_first(["a", "b"])  # type is Any, not str
result.upper()  # no error from mypy, even if result is None!

# CORRECT: use TypeVar to preserve type information
from typing import TypeVar, Optional
from collections.abc import Sequence

T = TypeVar("T")

def first(items: Sequence[T]) -> Optional[T]:
    return items[0] if items else None

result2 = first(["a", "b"])  # type is Optional[str]
# result2.upper()  # mypy error: Optional[str] may be None — must check first
if result2 is not None:
    result2.upper()  # OK, narrowed to str
Mistake 2: Forgetting that Generic list is invariant

class Animal: pass
class Dog(Animal): pass

def process_animals(animals: list[Animal]) -> None:
    animals.append(Animal())

dogs: list[Dog] = [Dog(), Dog()]
# WRONG: passing list[Dog] where list[Animal] is expected is a type error!
# (Mypy will flag this even though it seems intuitive)
# process_animals(dogs)  # mypy: error: "list[Dog]" incompatible with "list[Animal]"

# WHY: if this were allowed, process_animals could append an Animal
# (not a Dog) into dogs, making dogs: list[Dog] contain non-Dog items.

# CORRECT: use Sequence (covariant) for read-only containers
from collections.abc import Sequence

def process_animals_fixed(animals: Sequence[Animal]) -> None:
    for a in animals:
        print(type(a).__name__)

process_animals_fixed(dogs)  # OK! Sequence[Dog] <= Sequence[Animal]

# Or use the most general type needed:
from typing import Iterable
def count_animals(animals: Iterable[Animal]) -> int:
    return sum(1 for _ in animals)

count_animals(dogs)  # OK! Iterable[Dog] <= Iterable[Animal]
Mistake 3: Protocol doesn't check method signatures at runtime
Name TypeVars by their role, not just 'T'. Use
for collection element types. Single-letter TypeVars are fine for simple cases.

Define Protocols close to where they are used, not in a central 'interfaces' module. Protocols are lightweight and should express the minimal interface a specific caller needs — not a comprehensive description of all possible capabilities.
types (Sequence, Mapping, Iterable, Iterator, Callable) over concrete
, etc. for function inputs. This broadens the set of acceptable arguments and documents the minimal requirements.
sparingly — only for Protocols you explicitly need to isinstance-check at runtime. It adds a small overhead and the signature check limitation (runtime only checks name presence, not signature).

When defining generic classes, consider variance. Containers that are only read (getters) should use a covariant TypeVar; containers that are only written (setters) should use a contravariant TypeVar; mutable containers should use invariant (default).
when writing decorators that preserve the signature of the wrapped function. This is the correct way to annotate general decorators.
Generics and Protocols are entirely static constructs — they exist for the type checker and have no effect on runtime behavior or performance. A
is identical at runtime to a plain
do add a tiny overhead to

checks. The check examines whether the object's class or its MRO has the required methods by name, which involves attribute lookups. This is millisecond-order for a single check, negligible in almost all use cases.

import timeit

from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

@runtime_checkable
class HasLen(Protocol):
    def __len__(self) -> int:
        ...

# Comparing isinstance check vs regular isinstance
lst = [1, 2, 3]

# Protocol isinstance check
t1 = timeit.timeit(lambda: isinstance(lst, HasLen), number=100_000)
# Regular isinstance check
t2 = timeit.timeit(lambda: isinstance(lst, list), number=100_000)

print(f"Protocol isinstance: {t1:.3f}s per 100K calls")
print(f"Regular isinstance:  {t2:.3f}s per 100K calls")
# Protocol is ~10-20x slower but still ~microseconds per call
For mypy performance with complex generic hierarchies:
to enable incremental type checking. Unchanged modules are not re-checked.
Break large generic hierarchies into separate modules so mypy can check them in parallel.
Avoid deeply nested generic types (more than 3 levels deep). They are difficult to read and slow to check.
Understands Generic, TypeVar, Protocol, and all variance annotations. Use
to enforce that all TypeVars are used correctly. pip install mypy.
Often stricter than mypy on generic and Protocol compatibility. Particularly good at detecting generic type inference failures. pip install pyright.
The standard library's ABC/Protocol module defines Iterator, Iterable, Sequence, Mapping, MutableMapping, Callable, and many others. These are the building blocks for well-typed Python.
Backports newer typing features (ParamSpec, TypeVarTuple, Self, Unpack) to older Python versions. pip install typing_extensions.
syntax as a cleaner alternative to the TypeVar + Generic[T] pattern.
Generic and Protocol types improve reliability by catching interface mismatches at static-check time rather than at runtime. A Protocol-typed function that accepts any
object will fail at mypy-check time — not at deployment time — if called with an object that is missing the
If a class is supposed to implement a Protocol but is missing a method, mypy flags it when the class is first used as the Protocol type.
Generic type safety prevents category errors:
that stores strings cannot accidentally have integers inserted. The type system enforces the container's invariant.
Invariant generics (the default) prevent the classic 'stale container' bug where a

Runtime-checkable Protocol isinstance checks verify method name presence, not method security semantics. A class with a correctly-named method that has malicious behavior will pass the Protocol check.
Think of TypeVar and Generic as function-level templates, similar to C++ templates or Java generics, but resolved by the type checker rather than the compiler. When you write

, you are writing a template where T is a placeholder. The type checker fills in T concretely at every call site, as if you had written a separate function for each type. The key insight is that the type checker does all this work at check time — no code is generated, no runtime dispatch occurs.

Think of Protocol as 'programming to an interface without the interface.' Traditional OOP says: to accept any Drawable, make all Drawables inherit from an abstract Drawable class. Protocol says: to accept any Drawable, just describe what methods it needs. Anything that has those methods is acceptable. This is how Python has always worked at runtime (duck typing), but Protocol adds static verification.

The practical difference between ABC and Protocol becomes clear in a plugin system: with ABC, every plugin must import and subclass your ABC; with Protocol, every plugin just needs to implement the right methods. The Protocol approach works even for third-party classes you cannot modify.
TypeVar creates a type-level placeholder that is concretely filled in at each call site, enabling type-safe generic functions and classes that work with any type while preserving type information.
syntax) extends TypeVar to class-level parameterization, ensuring that all methods of a generic class consistently use the same type.
Protocol defines a structural interface without requiring inheritance: any class with the declared methods and attributes satisfies the Protocol, enabling duck typing with static verification.
types (Sequence, Iterable, Mapping) for function inputs instead of concrete types like
— these are already covariant or invariant Protocols that accept the widest range of compatible types.
Generic list and dict are invariant: a
even if Dog inherits from Animal — use
Protocol isinstance checks verify only method name presence, not signature — only mypy checks signatures, so never rely on runtime Protocol checks for security-critical decisions.
ParamSpec (Python 3.10+) is the correct tool for typing decorators that must preserve the full signature (positional args, keyword args, return type) of the wrapped function.
